explanation: Shea butter glycerides are a mixture of shea-derived monoglycerides, diglycerides, and triglycerides used as mild emollient and co-emulsifying agents with the nourishing fatty acid profile of shea butter. They are well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and provide barrier-supportive properties in moisturizing formulas.

Shea butter oleyl esters are shea butter fatty acids esterified with oleyl alcohol, producing a light emollient with excellent spreadability and a less occlusive skin feel than raw shea butter. They are well-tolerated and used in moisturizing formulas for a light, non-greasy shea-derived conditioning effect.

Shea butteramidopropyl betaine is an amphoteric betaine surfactant derived from shea butter fatty acids, used as a mild co-surfactant in cleansers that reduces primary-surfactant irritation while adding conditioning properties. It is well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and used in gentle cleansing formulas for its combined mild cleansing and shea-derived conditioning properties.

Shea butteramidopropyltrimonium chloride is a cationic quaternary ammonium conditioning agent derived from shea butter fatty acids, providing a substantive conditioning film on skin and hair surfaces for improved softness and slip. It is generally well-tolerated at typical cosmetic concentrations and used in conditioning formulas for its substantive shea-derived moisturization.

Silica (silicon dioxide, SiO₂) is used in cosmetics as a highly effective mattifying agent, oil absorbent, soft-focus texture modifier, and thickener, available in fine powder or colloidal form. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated; amorphous cosmetic silica is distinct from respirable crystalline silica and poses no inhalation concern at normal cosmetic use.

Silica dimethyl silylate is hydrophobically modified silica treated with dimethylsilyl groups, making it oil-dispersible and an effective oil-phase absorbent, thickener, and mattifying agent in anhydrous and sunscreen formulas. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated, functioning as an oil-phase texture and rheology modifier.

Silica silylate is a silylated form of silicon dioxide — similar to silica dimethyl silylate — used as a hydrophobic oil-phase thickener, absorbent, and texture modifier in anhydrous and sunscreen formulas. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated, functioning as an oil-phase rheology modifier.

Silk powder is finely milled Bombyx mori silk protein used in cosmetics as a soft-focus texture agent that provides a smooth, luminous, velvety skin finish. It is well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic concentrations, functioning as an optical and sensory modifier.

Simethicone is a mixture of polydimethylsiloxane and silica used primarily as an antifoaming agent that prevents bubble formation in formulas while providing a smooth, comfortable skin feel. It is considered well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and non-comedogenic at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Simmondsia chinensis esters are jojoba-derived ester fractions produced from Simmondsia chinensis (jojoba) seed wax, modified to produce specific ester variants with tailored skin feel and emollient properties. They are well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and used in moisturizing formulas for their skin-compatible jojoba-derived lipid profile.

Simmondsia chinensis seed oil (jojoba oil) is technically a liquid wax ester — not a true triglyceride oil — composed primarily of C20-C22 wax esters that closely resemble human sebum, providing exceptional skin compatibility, oxidative stability, and non-comedogenic emollient properties. It is exceptionally well-tolerated across all skin types including oily and acne-prone skin, and is one of the most shelf-stable plant oils due to its wax ester (not polyunsaturated fatty acid) composition.

Sinomonas/hyaluronic acid ferment filtrate is a fermentation filtrate produced by cultivating Sinomonas atrocyanea bacteria on a hyaluronic acid substrate, yielding a bioavailable mixture of low- and high-molecular-weight hyaluronan fragments with superior skin penetration compared to standard sodium hyaluronate. It is exceptionally well-tolerated and used in advanced hydrating formulas for its fermentation-derived, bioavailable hyaluronic acid content.

Sodium acetylated hyaluronate is an acetylated (lipid-modified) derivative of hyaluronic acid with increased lipophilicity compared to standard sodium hyaluronate, allowing it to penetrate more deeply into the lipid-rich intercellular matrix of the stratum corneum for more sustained hydration. It is exceptionally well-tolerated and used in premium moisturizing formulas as a high-performance lipophilic hyaluronate.

Sodium acrylate/sodium acryloyldimethyl taurate copolymer is a crosslinked synthetic acrylate-taurate copolymer used as an efficient thickener and rheology modifier in water-based formulas, providing a smooth, lightweight gel texture with good suspension and emulsion stability. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Sodium alginate is the sodium salt of alginic acid — a polysaccharide extracted from brown seaweed — used as a natural thickener, film-former, and texture modifier in skincare formulas. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations, with mild skin-conditioning properties from its polysaccharide structure.

Sodium ascorbyl phosphate is a stable, water-soluble vitamin C derivative that converts to ascorbic acid in the skin, providing antioxidant protection, brightening, and collagen-stimulating benefits with significantly better stability and lower irritation than pure L-ascorbic acid. It is exceptionally well-tolerated — including on sensitive and reactive skin — and is widely considered the preferred vitamin C derivative for sensitization-prone skin types.

Sodium benzoate is the sodium salt of benzoic acid used as a mild preservative effective against yeast and bacteria at acidic pH, commonly paired with citric acid or potassium sorbate for broader-spectrum protection. It is generally well-tolerated; when co-formulated with ascorbic acid (vitamin C), trace benzene formation is possible — a concern noted in formulas combining both ingredients.

The soft hyphen in this name is a data artifact from ingredient label parsing; this is the same ingredient as Sodium Benzoate — a mild preservative effective at acidic pH against yeast and bacteria. It is generally well-tolerated; trace benzene formation is possible when co-formulated with ascorbic acid.

Sodium bicarbonate (baking soda, NaHCO₃) is used in skincare as a pH adjuster, buffer, and mild deodorizing agent. Its relatively high alkalinity (pH ~8.3) means high concentrations can disrupt the skin's acid mantle, making it unsuitable for prolonged skin contact at high levels; in properly buffered finished formulas it is non-irritating.

Sodium C14-16 olefin sulfonate (alpha olefin sulfonate, AOS) is a synthetic surfactant providing effective cleansing with a milder irritation profile than sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS) in properly formulated products. It is generally well-tolerated at typical rinse-off concentrations; individuals with sulfate sensitivity should approach with caution.

Sodium cetearyl sulfate is a sulfate ester surfactant derived from cetearyl alcohol, used as an emulsifier and secondary surfactant in cleansers and emulsions. It is generally well-tolerated in rinse-off formulas; individuals with sulfate sensitivity may experience the same responses as with other sulfate surfactants.

Sodium chloride (table salt, NaCl) is used in skincare formulas as a texture modifier, thickener for surfactant systems, and isotonic tonicity agent in toners and mists. It is considered inert and well-tolerated at typical cosmetic concentrations; high salt concentrations on compromised or broken skin can be osmotically drying.

Sodium citrate is the sodium salt of citric acid used in skincare as a pH buffer and chelating agent that maintains formula stability while preventing metal-ion-catalyzed oxidation. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated at typical cosmetic use concentrations, functioning as a formulation stabilizer rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Sodium coceth sulfate is an ethoxylated coconut fatty alcohol sulfate surfactant used in cleansers, providing effective cleansing with somewhat milder irritation potential than non-ethoxylated coco-sulfate or SLS due to the ethoxylation. It is generally well-tolerated at rinse-off concentrations; individuals with sulfate sensitivity should note its composition.

Sodium coco-sulfate is a sulfate surfactant derived from coconut oil fatty acids — consisting primarily of sodium lauryl sulfate from coconut-origin lauric acid — providing effective cleansing with a similar irritation profile to SLS, despite being marketed as a natural coconut-derived alternative. Individuals with SLS or sulfate sensitivity should treat sodium coco-sulfate with the same caution.

Sodium cocoamphoacetate is a mild amphoteric surfactant derived from coconut fatty acids, used as a gentle co-surfactant in cleansers that reduces irritation from primary surfactants, improves lather quality, and adds conditioning. It is well-tolerated, low-sensitization, and widely used in gentle and baby cleansers for its excellent mildness profile.

Sodium cocoyl alaninate is a mild amino acid-derived surfactant made from coconut fatty acids and the amino acid alanine, providing gentle skin-compatible cleansing with a low irritation profile and excellent biodegradability. It is exceptionally well-tolerated — including on sensitive and eczema-prone skin — and used in premium, pH-balanced gentle cleansers for its amino acid-surfactant mildness.

Sodium cocoyl glycinate is a mild amino acid-derived surfactant made from coconut fatty acids and the amino acid glycine, providing gentle, skin-compatible cleansing at a naturally skin-friendly slightly acidic pH. It is well-tolerated, low-sensitization, and used in gentle and pH-balanced cleansers for its mild, biodegradable amino acid-surfactant profile.

Sodium cocoyl isethionate (SCI) is one of the mildest primary surfactants available — a coconut fatty acid isethionate ester used in bar cleansers and gentle washes providing effective cleansing with the lowest irritation potential among common primary surfactants, even at high concentrations. It is exceptionally well-tolerated including on sensitive, atopic, and rosacea-prone skin, and is the gold-standard surfactant in the mildest cleansing formulas.

Sodium hyaluronate is the sodium salt of hyaluronic acid — one of the most ubiquitous and reliable humectants in skincare — which forms a water-binding film on the skin surface to provide immediate hydration and a plumping effect, with penetration depth varying by molecular weight. It is exceptionally well-tolerated by all skin types including reactive and compromised skin, and is among the most reliably safe and effective humectants in modern skincare.
